topic: A neighbor wants to install a fence
raw_output:
[story 1]

The hammering started at seven in the morning, a relentless, rhythmic assault on the peace of my Saturday. I was attempting to enjoy a quiet cup of coffee on my porch, the sun warming my face, when the first sharp thwack echoed from across the lawn. My eyes snapped open. It was Mr. Henderson, my neighbor, wielding a post-hole digger with alarming enthusiasm. I quickly retreated inside, my hands gripping the mug so tightly my knuckles turned white. Every sound—the scraping metal, the dull thud of wood—seemed amplified in the sudden silence of my living room. I kept picturing the construction encroaching on my yard, the visual disruption, and a hot flush crept up my neck. I spent the rest of the afternoon staring blankly at the wall, unable to focus on anything but the impending boundary change.

[story 2]

"Morning!" Mr. Henderson called out, his voice booming across the shared fence line. I flinched, nearly dropping my newspaper. I’d been trying to have a peaceful morning, reading the news, but his sudden, loud announcement shattered the calm. I could feel a distinct tightening in my chest, a strange, hot pressure behind my sternum. When I finally ventured out to the edge of my property, the sight of him, armed with tools and a determined look, made my mouth go dry. I mumbled a hasty, "Good morning," and immediately turned my back, walking quickly toward the sanctuary of my garage, desperate to become invisible. I kept replaying the interaction in my head, wishing the pavement would swallow me whole.

[story 3]

I was meticulously tending to my rose bushes, trying to channel my anxiety into focused, repetitive motion. Then, the sound began—the unmistakable scrape of a shovel against the earth, followed by the distinct, heavy sound of a post being driven. It was Mr. Henderson again, and this time, he was clearly making significant progress. A wave of intense discomfort washed over me; my breath hitched, and I instinctively dropped the trowel. I stood frozen by the hedge, watching the activity with wide, unblinking eyes. My palms began to sweat, slicking my skin. I wanted to vanish, to melt into the shadow of the bushes, wishing desperately that the ground would open up and consume me whole.
